food_name: Rice Flour (plain, raw, from long-grain white rice)

ontology.foodex2_description: Rice flour, white, milled from long-grain white rice

classification.processing_level: processed (white rice milled into fine flour)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical recipe portion of rice flour is 30g (approximately 3 tablespoons). In idiyappam making, about 100–150g flour is used to make a full plate serving.

nutrient_source.title: Rice Flour — HPB Singapore Foodo Database, Food Code F05040011; cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore; USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Exact match in HPB Foodo under food code F05040011 — Rice Flour, plain white, raw.

gi_source: University of Sydney GI Database — GI of rice flour-based preparations (rice porridge, congee ~78). Dry rice flour GI not applicable. Rice flour has a high GI when cooked (products range 70–95 depending on preparation).

hypertension.key_factor: Rice flour contains negligible sodium (0mg/100g) and has no direct effect on blood pressure. Its nutritional profile is similar to white rice with slightly higher starch content.
hypertension.guidance: Rice flour-based preparations (kueh, idiyappam) are suitable for hypertensive patients. Blood pressure concerns arise from added ingredients (coconut milk, salt, sauces) rather than the flour itself.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Rice flour has a very high GI when cooked (~78+). Products made from rice flour (idiyappam, kueh, rice noodles) can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation. Lower fibre than wholegrain alternatives.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should moderate consumption of rice flour-based foods. When used in cooking, combining with protein and fat helps blunt the glycaemic response. For baking and kueh-making, partial substitution with almond flour or chickpea flour reduces the GI.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Rice flour is gluten-free, making it a safe grain alternative for the subset of rheumatoid arthritis patients who are also gluten-sensitive. It provides only modest anti-inflammatory benefit.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Rice flour is an acceptable gluten-free flour choice. For patients with rheumatoid arthritis seeking more anti-inflammatory grain options, almond or chickpea flour provides additional micronutrients.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Rice flour is a fine white powder milled from long-grain white rice. Completely gluten-free. It is the primary flour used in many Southeast Asian and South Indian preparations: idiyappam (string hoppers), murukku, kueh (Singaporean Chinese cakes), rice paper, and bee hoon (rice vermicelli). Nutritionally similar to white rice — high in starch, low in protein and fibre compared to wheat flour. The GI when cooked is high (~78+). Distinct from glutinous rice flour (made from waxy rice) — the two are not interchangeable in recipes as they produce different textures.

notes: Values are per 100g dry rice flour. Distinct from glutinous (waxy) rice flour — these are not interchangeable. Rice flour has no gluten and produces non-elastic doughs. High starch content gives baked/steamed products a chewy or crumbly texture depending on the water ratio.
